narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and—countering that somewhat—your upper-middle family income bracket. Most strongly influencing the outcome was your placement in the second decile for Year 3 grades, which is notably lower than the mid-to-upper deciles typical among students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance through law school. Close behind was your LSAT score of 25, well below the average of about 37 for those who succeeded on the bar, as lower scores have historically correlated with higher failure rates in this dataset. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income stood out as a supportive factor, aligning with the majority of passers (around 45%) and pushing slightly toward a success prediction compared to lower brackets. For context, students admitted typically show stronger profiles here—think Year 3 deciles around 6, LSATs near 37, and undergraduate GPAs averaging 3.24 versus your 2.50—putting you below those benchmarks in key academic areas despite your full-time study plans matching the norm. These elements combined to tip the scales toward rejection, as the model prioritizes bar passage as a core indicator of fit for our program. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's a data-driven risk assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth since Year 3. Many students in similar positions have improved these metrics and succeeded elsewhere; reaching out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback could help refine your next application. With focused effort on those academic standouts, you can shift the odds in your favor.
